does: This paper develops susceptibilities, an interpretability technique that measures how posterior expectations change in response to perturbations of the loss function, and applies this framework to deep reinforcement learning by generalizing it from supervised learning to the regret minimization setting. The authors demonstrate susceptibilities on a gridworld environment that exhibits non-trivial learning dynamics across training stages, showing that the technique can reveal internal parameter-space features during model development that remain invisible when examining only the learned policy behavior. They validate their findings through activation-steering experiments and discuss how susceptibilities could extend to RLHF post-training scenarios.
connects: This work builds on susceptibilities as a neural network interpretability tool originally developed for supervised learning, extending a technique that probes model internals through loss perturbations into the reinforcement learning domain. It engages with the broader literature on RL agent interpretability and mechanistic understanding, which has increasingly sought methods beyond policy-level analysis to understand how agents develop behavioral and representational strategies during training. The paper connects to research on mechanistic interpretability in deep networks and represents an effort to apply formal perturbation-based analysis techniques to the more complex optimization landscape of reinforcement learning where regret rather than supervised loss is the primary objective.
enables: By establishing susceptibilities as a diagnostic tool for RL training dynamics, this work opens pathways for more fine-grained understanding of how deep RL agents develop skills and representations during training. The framework could facilitate detection of learned features and internal representations that contribute to or detract from desired behavior, potentially supporting safety analysis in RL systems. The extension to RLHF mentioned in the abstract suggests applications to interpreting large language models during preference-based fine-tuning, where understanding which model components respond to alignment training could inform how to steer or modify learned behaviors. This could support development of more transparent and controllable RL and aligned AI systems.
